title: CredibleDFGO: Differentiable Factor Graph Optimization with Credibility Supervision
abstract: 全球导航卫星系统(GNSS)定位广泛应用于城市导航，但在城市峡谷环境中，GNSS解算器提供的协方差估计往往不可靠。现有可微因子图优化(DFGO)方法虽已通过解算器学习测量权重，但仅采用位置级目标函数。这导致均值估计可能改善，而协方差估计仍存在偏小、偏大或形态失真的问题。本文提出可置信DFGO(CredibleDFGO, CDFGO)框架，该可微GNSS因子图框架将协方差可信度作为显式训练目标。权重生成网络(WGN)预测每颗卫星的可靠性权重，可微高斯-牛顿解算器将这些权重映射为位置估计与后验协方差，并通过恰当评分规则对东-北向预测分布进行端到端监督。我们研究了负对数似然(NLL)、能量评分(ES)及其组合策略。在三个UrbanNav测试场景上的实验表明，不确定性可信度获得一致性提升。中城市场景与强城市场景中的定位精度同步改善，深城市场景中的平均水平误差和95百分位误差亦有所优化。在强城市化的旺角(MK)场景中，CDFGO-Combined将平均水平误差从13.77米降至11.68米，NLL从40.63降至6.59，ES从12.31降至9.05。案例研究将MK场景的性能提升归因于更优的轴向一致性、更可信的局部协方差椭圆以及卫星级权重重分配。

CredibleDFGO: Differentiable Factor Graph Optimization with Credibility Supervision
Liang Qian
Penggao Yan
Penghui Xu
Li-Ta Hsu
Department of Aeronautical and Aviation Engineering, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hong Kong, China
lit.hsu@polyu.edu.hk
(–)
Abstract
[Abstract]Global navigation satellite system (GNSS) positioning is widely used for urban navigation, but the covariance reported by the GNSS solver is often unreliable in urban canyons. Existing differentiable factor graph optimization (DFGO) methods already learn measurement weighting through the solver, but they still use position-only objectives. As a result, the mean estimate may improve while the reported covariance remains too small, too large, or wrong in shape. In this work, we propose CredibleDFGO (CDFGO), a differentiable GNSS factor graph framework that makes covariance credibility an explicit training target. The Weighting Generation Network (WGN) predicts per-satellite reliability weights. The differentiable Gauss–Newton solver maps these weights to a position estimate and posterior covariance, and proper scoring rules supervise the East–North predictive distribution end-to-end. We study negative log-likelihood (NLL), Energy Score (ES), and their combination. Results on three UrbanNav test scenes show consistent gains in uncertainty credibility. Positioning accuracy also improves on the medium-urban and harsh-urban scenes, and the mean horizontal error and 95th-percentile error improve on the deep-urban scene. On the harsh-urban Mong Kok (MK) scene, CDFGO-Combined reduces the mean horizontal error from 13.77 m to 11.68 m, reduces NLL from 40.63 to 6.59, and reduces ES from 12.31 to 9.05. The case studies link the MK improvement to better axis-wise consistency, more credible local covariance ellipses, and satellite-level reweighting.

Introduction
GNSS positioning in urban canyons can return an accurate position, but the reported covariance does not match the true position error. This mismatch matters because the covariance is not just a diagnostic number. It directly controls downstream decisions. The covariance controls how much fusion modules trust the GNSS position update and when integrity monitors raise an alert. A miscalibrated covariance misleads both. Li and colleagues gave this problem a name: credibility
[
23
]
. Credibility assesses whether the reported uncertainty is statistically consistent with the true position error. Position accuracy alone does not tell us whether the reported uncertainty was credible. Yan and colleagues recently showed that two proper scoring rules, negative log-likelihood (NLL) and the energy score (ES), can detect different kinds of covariance miscalibration in state estimators under noise and model mismatch
[
48
]
. These different kinds of miscalibration correspond to three concrete failure modes. The reported ellipse may be too small, too large, or pointing in the wrong direction. In horizontal positioning, a tight ellipse can still fail to cover the true East–North position. Two downstream systems show why this risk is not just theoretical. Joerger and Pervan showed that sequential advanced receiver autonomous integrity monitoring (ARAIM) can exploit satellite motion over time only when the uncertainty model stays credible
[
13
]
. In driverless urban navigation, Nagai and colleagues found that fault-free integrity requires credible GNSS uncertainty for fusion with other onboard sensors
[
28
]
. Therefore, credibility cannot be inferred from the position error. It must be assessed directly from the reported covariance. This requirement applies to any estimator family — classical weighted least squares, robust filters, or learned factor graphs.
Yet most work in urban GNSS targets position accuracy, not covariance credibility. Most methods work on the measurements before they reach the solver. Zheng and colleagues predicted satellite visibility from a sky-plot graph using a graph transformer
[
51
]
. Building on 3D city models, Wang and colleagues distinguished LOS from NLOS signals using shadow matching
[
38
]
. Ng and colleagues also extended 3D-mapping-aided GNSS to dual-frequency smartphone pseudoranges
[
29
]
. Li and colleagues learned NLOS-aware weights from signal features, so contaminated and clean measurements are weighted differently
[
22
]
. Mohanty and Gao used graph convolutions to learn smartphone GNSS corrections from inter-satellite relations
[
26
]
. Smolyakov and colleagues adapted the stochastic model online through multipath prediction and detection
[
34
]
. A more direct approach by Chen and colleagues learned pseudorange corrections that reduce urban bias directly
[
2
]
. Kanhere and colleagues trained a set transformer to predict position corrections from pseudorange residuals and line-of-sight vectors
[
15
]
. Zhang and colleagues took a different direction by making measurement uncertainty itself the prediction target, using an LSTM to model its time-varying behavior
[
50
]
. Medina and colleagues reviewed classical robust GNSS estimators under harsh conditions, showing that standard least squares becomes unreliable when outliers and heavy tails dominate
[
25
]
. Even a dedicated urban M-estimator was found to be sensitive to the choice of the error model, and its performance depended on proper design
[
6
]
. Li and colleagues tuned the Huber M-estimator parameters using the logistic distribution of GNSS errors, reducing the 2D RMSE and suppressing large error spikes
[
24
]
. Yan and colleagues isolated multi-constellation faults through incremental expansion of a fault-free basic set
[
46
]
. All of these methods target the measurements or the robust estimator before the solver runs. The covariance the solver returns is never their supervision target.
The same gap exists within the factor‑graph framework. Several methods address measurement faults and integrity monitoring, but they still do not supervise the covariance. Wen, Hsu, and colleagues brought factor-graph optimization into robust GNSS and RTK positioning
[
40
]
. They later applied graduated non-convexity to downweight pseudorange outliers
[
42
]
, and a related thread from the same group fused GNSS and LiDAR with self-adaptive Gaussian mixtures
[
39
]
. Yan and colleagues learned subspace-based adaptive GMM error models for fault-aware urban positioning
[
47
]
. Knowles and Gao developed rapid fault detection and exclusion
[
19
]
. For long-term integrity, Gallon and colleagues hardened orbit and clock error modeling
[
5
]
. Xia and colleagues came closest to the covariance credibility question. Their integrity-constrained factor graph uses switch variables to reweight measurements and derives a protection level from the modified weighting matrix, though their method does not involve learning
[
43
]
. Their work shows that the factor-graph framework can engage with covariance, but it needs a training signal that targets it. Without such a signal, even general-purpose uncertainty quantification tools face the same limitation. Gal and Ghahramani’s Monte Carlo dropout approximates Bayesian uncertainty by sampling subnetworks, but the factor-graph solver already provides a posterior covariance from the inverse of the converged Hessian
[
4
]
. Levi and colleagues showed how to calibrate regression uncertainty after training, but post-hoc calibration does not change which satellites receive high weight during the forward pass
[
21
]
. None of these approaches train the covariance to match the true position error.
A different route is needed to supervise the covariance. In a differentiable factor graph, the solver is unrolled so that gradients flow from the loss back through the optimization to the factor weights. Because the posterior covariance is produced by the Hessian of the converged solution, a credibility loss can directly penalize a miscalibrated covariance through the same backward pass.
This idea builds on decades of work on factor graphs for robot perception. Dellaert and Kaess formulated factor graphs and smoothing as a sparse probabilistic framework that incorporates measurements, priors, and constraints inside one optimization problem
[
3
]
. The iSAM2 and Bayes tree formulations from Kaess and colleagues made incremental smoothing practical at navigation scale
[
14
]
, and Taylor and Gross recently framed the whole family as a navigation generalization of Kalman filtering for systems that mix asynchronous sensors
[
35
]
. Several groups have also tried to embed learning inside GNSS estimators. Mohanty and Gao connected a graph neural network to a backpropagation Kalman filter so that the learned corrections directly affect the state estimate
[
27
]
. Hu and colleagues coupled deep bias and weight prediction with RTKLIB-style positioning
[
12
]
. In these designs, the learning signal reaches the estimator output and gradients flow through the estimator. However, the training loss still targets only the position estimate. Mohanty and Gao note explicitly that they leave covariance learning for future work. These designs make the estimator differentiable but do not unroll the factor graph optimization itself. Yi and colleagues addressed this by unrolling the optimization steps and backpropagating through them
[
49
]
, establishing DFGO as a training paradigm. The idea of differentiating through a solved optimization problem is not limited to navigation. Amos and Kolter treated optimization as a neural-network layer in OptNet and derived exact gradients through solved programs
[
1
]
. Xu and colleagues’ DFGO-ICov brought this idea to smartphone GNSS, with a network predicting adaptive covariance scales for pseudorange factors and a differentiable solver training these scales against ground-truth position
[
45
]
. Their follow-up AutoW replaced ground-truth labels with self-supervised priors from clustering and zero-velocity constraints, reporting gains over classical weighting schemes
[
44
]
. But the training loss in these methods still acts on the position estimate, not the posterior covariance. A position-only loss cannot distinguish a covariance that is tight and correct from one that is tight but misoriented, because both can yield the same position estimate.
A loss that only sees the position error cannot judge whether the covariance is right. To evaluate both the position estimate and the posterior covariance, the loss must act on the full predictive distribution that the solver outputs. Proper scoring rules do exactly this. A strictly proper scoring rule attains its lowest expected value only when the predicted distribution matches the true distribution. This means a covariance that looks precise but is poorly calibrated is penalized, not rewarded. For the two-dimensional East–North prediction, scoring each axis independently fails to capture the correlation between the two axes
[
7
,
8
,
33
]
. Yan and colleagues already used these scoring rules as credibility metrics for state estimators
[
48
]
. CDFGO uses the same rules and turns them into training losses inside a differentiable solver. For the East–North Gaussian predictor used in GNSS, NLL penalizes a position estimate that is far from the true position and a covariance ellipse that is too small, too large, or pointing in the wrong direction. The energy score works differently: it measures the expected distance from predictive samples to the true position, minus half the expected distance between pairs of predictive samples. Because the energy score does not assume that the errors follow a Gaussian distribution, it remains informative when the urban pseudorange errors are heavy-tailed. Under a credible Gaussian predictor, about 68% of axis-wise errors should lie within the reported 1-
σ
\sigma
bound and about 99.7% within the 3-
σ
\sigma
bound. CredibleDFGO (CDFGO) implements this credibility supervision end-to-end. A Weighting Generation Network (WGN) maps per-satellite features to reliability weights, which a differentiable Gauss–Newton solver
[
30
]
uses as per-factor information weights, and the gradients flow directly through the solver back to the WGN parameters
[
1
]
. We study three variants. CDFGO-NLL optimizes the Gaussian NLL of the East–North posterior, CDFGO-ES optimizes the energy score, and CDFGO-Combined trains against both. We evaluate CDFGO on three UrbanNav test scenes covering medium-urban, deep-urban, and harsh-urban conditions. Credibility-driven weighting can suppress contaminated satellites that still contribute to the geometry, so this sometimes results in weaker geometry that yields a covariance that better matches the true position error. This geometry-credibility tradeoff is a core feature of the method, not a side effect.
The contributions of this paper are threefold:
•
We show that proper scoring rules, when used as end-to-end training losses for a differentiable GNSS factor graph, make the posterior covariance a supervised output rather than an unsupervised byproduct.
•
We develop the CredibleDFGO framework and demonstrate on three UrbanNav scenes that credibility-driven training improves uncertainty credibility while preserving or improving horizontal accuracy.
•
We identify a geometry-credibility tradeoff inherent in credibility-driven weighting. A satellite-level analysis on the Mong Kok scene shows how this tradeoff reshapes the posterior covariance and why it still improves both accuracy and credibility.

2.2
CDFGO Pipeline Overview
Figure
1
summarizes the CDFGO pipeline: the WGN, the differentiable Gauss–Newton solver, and credibility supervision.
During training, the WGN converts raw GNSS features into reliability weights. These weights define the pseudorange-factor information used by the differentiable Gauss–Newton solver, which returns the state estimate and posterior covariance. Credibility losses are then computed from the East–North position and covariance returned by the solver, and the loss gradients are back-propagated through the solver to the WGN parameters
ϕ

\tilde{\phi}
.
During inference, only the trained WGN and the solver are used. The runtime solver therefore stays the same as in a standard FGO backend; only the factor weights are learned from data.

Overview of CDFGO. Panel (A) shows the WGN, which maps raw GNSS features to per-factor weights. Panel (B) shows the differentiable factor-graph solver, which uses these weights to produce state estimates and posterior covariance. Panel (C) shows the credibility supervision stage, where NLL/ES-based losses are back-propagated through the whole pipeline.

3
Experiments and Mechanism Analysis
3.1
Dataset and Experiment Setup
In this work, we use the UrbanNav dataset
[
10
]
, which is a public multi-sensor urban-navigation dataset in Hong Kong covering medium- to harsh-urban scenes with RTK/INS reference trajectories, to evaluate the performance of the proposed method. Specifically, three test scenes are used: Tsim Sha Tsui (TST), Whampoa (WP), and Mong Kok (MK), representing Medium Urban, Deep Urban, and Harsh Urban conditions, respectively. Figure
2
shows aerial overviews of the three test scenes, illustrating how building density and street canyon depth increase from TST (medium urban) to WP (deep urban) to MK (harsh urban). All recordings use the same hardware setup, with a u-blox ZED-F9P receiver for raw GNSS measurements and a NovAtel SPAN-CPT RTK/INS system for the reference trajectory.

The mean reflects the overall operating level, the median the typical-case performance, and the 95th percentile the tail behavior.

3
compares point accuracy and covariance credibility across the four baselines and three CredibleDFGO variants on the TST, WP, and MK scenes. On the medium-urban TST scene, all three CredibleDFGO variants outperform DFGO (MAE) in both point accuracy and credibility. CDFGO-ES gives the lowest mean horizontal error at 4.71 m, compared with 6.27 m for DFGO (MAE), and also gives the lowest 95th-percentile error at 11.86 m. CDFGO-Combined gives the lowest NLL, reducing it from 14.06 to 3.67, a 73.9% reduction. The three credibility-driven variants also reduce ES by similar margins, which shows that credibility supervision improves distribution-level performance on this medium-urban scene.
On the deep-urban WP scene, the pattern is different. DFGO (MAE) gives the lowest median error, so credibility-driven training does not improve the median. The main accuracy gain appears in the tail: CDFGO-Combined reduces the 95th-percentile error from 22.86 m to 17.39 m, a 23.9% reduction, and lowers the mean horizontal error from 6.99 m to 6.44 m. The credibility metrics also improve, with NLL reduced from 13.08 to 3.76 and ES reduced from 5.92 to 4.61. CDFGO-NLL gives a slightly lower 95th-percentile error at 17.29 m. On WP, credibility-driven training mainly improves tail behavior and covariance credibility rather than the median error.
On the harsh-urban MK scene, the separation between DFGO (MAE) and the credibility-driven objectives is largest. CDFGO-Combined reduces the mean horizontal error from 13.77 m to 11.68 m, a 15.2% reduction, and reduces NLL from 40.63 to 6.59. ES also drops from 12.31 to 9.05, and the 95th-percentile error drops from 36.55 m to 33.26 m. The median error improves from 10.04 m to 7.89 m, while CDFGO-NLL gives the lowest median at 7.60 m. Because MK shows the largest NLL reduction and joint gains in accuracy and credibility in Table
3
, the mechanism analysis below focuses on DFGO (MAE) and CDFGO-Combined on this scene.

4
Discussion and Limitations
The experiment results in Section
3
indicate that credibility training mainly changes how the solver’s covariance can be used downstream. In the present setting, covariance is no longer a passive byproduct of the solver; it becomes an explicit training target. This matters because downstream fusion and integrity modules act on covariance, not on horizontal error alone. In multi-sensor fusion, covariance controls the update gain and therefore how strongly GNSS pulls the fused estimate
[
41
]
. In integrity-oriented processing, uncertainty quality is itself part of the decision logic
[
43
]
. A GNSS backend that reports more credible covariance is therefore a stronger candidate for downstream fusion and integrity integration, even though those downstream systems are not evaluated in this work.
The credibility-oriented objectives also provide a direct training-time diagnostic. Because the uncertainty behavior is tied to an explicit proper score, a model update can be judged not only by aggregate positioning error but also by whether the reported covariance remains statistically consistent with realized errors, which is exactly the role of proper scoring rules such as NLL
[
7
]
. The exceedance and coverage diagnostics in Section
3.4
serve the same purpose for credibility checking
[
23
]
. They do not provide formal certification, but they do reveal whether the uncertainty behavior is moving toward statistical consistency. This framing is closer to integrity-oriented evaluation than to optimizing geometry-oriented losses alone.
At the same time, this study has clear limits. The loss balancing still uses fixed coefficients
(
α
,
β
)
(\alpha,\beta)
, although the calibration-sharpness trade-off can vary with scene difficulty and mission profile. The posterior covariance is still extracted from a local Laplace approximation, which is efficient but may be insufficient when the posterior is strongly multi-modal. These two points suggest natural extensions toward adaptive loss balancing and richer uncertainty families.
The experimental scope is also deliberately narrow. The reported results use pseudorange-only factors. This isolates the mechanism of credibility training, but it does not show how the same credibility-driven training objective behaves after coupling with Doppler, carrier phase, or inertial constraints. Extending the framework to tightly coupled GNSS/INS or GNSS/vision graphs is a necessary next step for studying downstream fusion and integrity behavior
[
41
,
43
]
. The mechanism interpretation in Section
3.6
is also built around a representative MK epoch. This selected epoch is useful for explanation, but it does not establish the same causal pattern across all epochs and scenes. Larger-scale satellite-level statistics are still needed, and developing such statistics is a clear direction for future work.
The present evaluation is further limited by geography, hardware, and reference quality. UrbanNav covers several urban scenes
[
10
]
, but its reference trajectories are tied to a single hardware setup and to high-grade RTK/INS post-processing that may still retain some uncertainty under severe blockage. Because the credibility losses depend on reference quality, label errors can transfer into the learned weighting behavior. End-to-end differentiable optimization with Monte Carlo ES estimation is also more expensive than hand-crafted weighting rules, and gradient stability still depends on solver convergence. Broader cross-domain experiments, more diverse hardware platforms, and more efficient training strategies are therefore needed before deployment-oriented use can be claimed.
5
Conclusion
In this work, we introduced CDFGO, a differentiable GNSS factor graph trained with proper-scoring-rule losses on its East–North predictive distribution. Experiments on the UrbanNav dataset across medium, deep, and harsh urban scenes confirmed that credibility supervision produces a more reliable posterior covariance and improves horizontal accuracy. In contrast, the DFGO (MAE) baseline with a position-only loss returns miscalibrated covariance even when its position estimate is accurate. This work offers GNSS practitioners a way to train covariance as a primary estimator output, enabling direct use in downstream fusion and integrity monitoring.

preview_text:
Global navigation satellite system (GNSS) positioning is widely used for urban navigation, but the covariance reported by the GNSS solver is often unreliable in urban canyons. Existing differentiable factor graph optimization (DFGO) methods already learn measurement weighting through the solver, but they still use position-only objectives. As a result, the mean estimate may improve while the reported covariance remains too small, too large, or wrong in shape. In this work, we propose CredibleDFGO (CDFGO), a differentiable GNSS factor graph framework that makes covariance credibility an explicit training target. The Weighting Generation Network (WGN) predicts per-satellite reliability weights. The differentiable Gauss--Newton solver maps these weights to a position estimate and posterior covariance, and proper scoring rules supervise the East--North predictive distribution end-to-end. We study negative log-likelihood (NLL), Energy Score (ES), and their combination. Results on three UrbanNav test scenes show consistent gains in uncertainty credibility. Positioning accuracy also improves on the medium-urban and harsh-urban scenes, and the mean horizontal error and 95th-percentile error improve on the deep-urban scene. On the harsh-urban Mong Kok (MK) scene, CDFGO-Combined reduces the mean horizontal error from 13.77\,m to 11.68\,m, reduces NLL from 40.63 to 6.59, and reduces ES from 12.31 to 9.05. The case studies link the MK improvement to better axis-wise consistency, more credible local covariance ellipses, and satellite-level reweighting.
